coredata.dc:title: N/S-RCQD@NiCo2S4 nanocomposite with wrinkled nanosheet-like edges as an anode for water splitting
coredata.dc:creator: Amjadi, Mahdiyyeh Sadeghi
Ashassi-Sorkhabi, Habib
Hosseini, Mir Ghasem
Pollet, Bruno G.
Asghari, Elnaz
coredata.dc:description: The hybrid of carbon quantum dot nanoparticles wrapped at NiCo2S4 flower-like with nanosheets-like edges is introduced as a desirable nonprecious electrocatalyst for the oxygen evolution reaction, OER, in the alkaline medium. Co (CH3COO)2, Ni(CH3COO)2, thiourea, and carbon quantum dots are used as precursors, with ethylene glycol as a dispersant. Reduced carbon quantum dots (RCQD) are doped with sulfur and nitrogen traces. The core-shell structure increases the contact area between the material and the electrolyte, resulting in higher electrochemical performance. Doping of N/S-RCQD to NiCo2S4 results in improved electrocatalytic performance and stability. The N/S-RCQD@NiCo2S4 only requires a low overpotential of 390 mV for OER at 10 mA.cm−2, owing to many exposed active sites and facilitated diffusion. Furthermore, in 0.1 M KOH for the OER, the N/S-RCQD@NiCo2S4 exhibits outstanding long-term durability. The structure and morphology of N/S-RCQD@NiCo2S4 are studied by field emission scanning electron microscopy, X-ray diffraction, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, Fourier Transform Infrared spectroscopy, UV–Vis absorption and photoluminescence excitation and photoluminescence emission. The core-shell structure facilitates molecular diffusion and adsorption of species. The synergistic effect of Ni and Co also enhances the catalytic properties.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The hybrid of carbon quantum dot nanoparticles wrapped at NiCo2S4 flower-like with nanosheets-like edges is introduced as a desirable nonprecious electrocatalyst for the oxygen evolution reaction, OER, in the alkaline medium. Co (CH3COO)2, Ni(CH3COO)2, thiourea, and carbon quantum dots are used as precursors, with ethylene glycol as a dispersant. Reduced carbon quantum dots (RCQD) are doped with sulfur and nitrogen traces. The core-shell structure increases the contact area between the material and the electrolyte, resulting in higher electrochemical performance. Doping of N/S-RCQD to NiCo2S4 results in improved electrocatalytic performance and stability. The N/S-RCQD@NiCo2S4 only requires a low overpotential of 390 mV for OER at 10 mA.cm−2, owing to many exposed active sites and facilitated diffusion. Furthermore, in 0.1 M KOH for the OER, the N/S-RCQD@NiCo2S4 exhibits outstanding long-term durability. The structure and morphology of N/S-RCQD@NiCo2S4 are studied by field emission scanning electron microscopy, X-ray diffraction, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, Fourier Transform Infrared spectroscopy, UV–Vis absorption and photoluminescence excitation and photoluminescence emission. The core-shell structure facilitates molecular diffusion and adsorption of species. The synergistic effect of Ni and Co also enhances the catalytic properties.

ce:keyword.ce:text: S co-doped reduced carbon quantum dot

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: In the field of growing energy consumption and related environmental damage issues, electrochemical water splitting is a suitable approach to produce clean, high energy density, and sustainable H2 fuel [1]. The two half-reactions, the oxygen evolution reaction (OER) at the anode and the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) at the cathode, make up electrochemical water splitting [2]. However, the slow kinetics OER reduces the efficiency of water-splitting devices significantly, which necessitates the use of efficient OER catalysts [3,4]. Iridium (Ir) and ruthenium (Ru) oxides are the most efficient advanced OER catalysts. [5–8]. However, they are costly and scarce, making them unsuitable for large-scale applications [7,9]. For this purpose, it is critical to find low-cost and abundant alternatives on the ground [10]. As OER electrocatalysts, several non-noble transition metal chalcogenides (TMCs) have been investigated recently [11–13]. For example, ternary component catalysts have attracted attention due to the mutually beneficial synergy between several components and complex chemical composition [14–16]. In particular, the Co3+/Co2+ and Ni3+/Ni2+ redox couples found in the Ni-Co-based catalyst are effective active centers for OER. NiCo2S4 bimetallic atoms have a synergistic effect that provides a considerable number of active redox sites, higher electrical conductivity, and higher capacitance with exceptional cycling stability than single-component oxides and sulfides; they are often used in supercapacitor and batteries [17–19]. However, there need to be more research on NiCo2S4 as an electrocatalyst due to its tiny specific surface area, poor dynamics, structure instability and destruction during the OER process [20]. Several attempts have been made to improve the specific surface area and reveal more active sites to increase the catalytic efficacy. Enhancing the catalytic activity of water splitting by including effective catalysts on heteroatom -doped carbon materials (such as heteroatom-incorporated graphene, carbon nanorod and carbon nanotubes etc.) can significantly increase the number of active sites and ease the diffusion of active species and electron transport [3,21–23]. Many efforts have been made to replace those above expensive carbon-based materials and investigate new inexpensive carbon-based materials whose impact could lead to potential enhancements in electrochemical performance as hybrid electrode materials. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a new class of zero-dimensional (0D) carbon nanomaterials with a diameter of less than 10 nm that have attracted academic and industrial attention [24]. Because of their good aqueous solubility, outstanding performance, low toxicity, exceptional electrical conductivity, high chemical stability, and environmental friendliness, CQDs have been extensively researched as a foundation for making transition metal chalcogenides hybrids for a variety of applications [25,26]. Since the hydroxyl and carboxyl segments on the surface of the CQDs can act as electron acceptors and donors during various reaction processes, They are used as oxidants or reductants [27,28]. The functional group of CQDs can also make them nucleation centers for metallic nanoparticle growth [29]. For example, in solid-state nanostructured solar cells, ZnO nanorods/CQDs hybrid materials have been used as electrodes; they demonstrated better catalytic activity than pure ZnO, which is attributed to the unique features of CQDs [30]. Zhao et al. [31], on the other hand, used a simple hydrothermal approach to make 0D CQDs/2D MOS2 composites, which are mostly made up of sandwiched S–Mo–S layers and are used as catalysts in the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER). This new type of electrocatalyst displayed more efficient electrocatalytic performance towards HER after being exposed to visible light for 30 min. It had a low overpotential of 0.125 V at 10 mA.cm−2, a high current density, a slight Tafel slope of 45 mV/decade, and high stability in 0.5 M H2SO4. Heteroatom doping can alter the structure of carbon materials, resulting in better intrinsic qualities such as electronic structures, electron conductivity, and chemical state [24]. For example, nitrogen-doped carbon materials can create a negative charge on the delocalized sp2-hybridized carbon framework, leading to an increased electron transfer [32]. We present a Ni-Co-S bimetallic catalyst in which the reduced carbon quantum dots (RCQD) acts as a synergistic component. In situ, hybrid, N/S-RCQD nanoparticles are wrapped at the nanosheet-like edges of NiCo2S4, forming the nanocomposite (N/S-RCQD@ NiCo2S4) was synthesized from metal salts, thiourea, and carbon quantum dots in ethylene glycol (EG) medium using a simple one-pot solvothermal process. Compared with NiCo2S4 without N/S-RCQDs and N/S-RCQD@Co3S4, it was found that the N/S-RCQD@NiCo2S4 nanostructure showed outstanding catalytic efficacy towards OER.

Ni(CH3CO2)2·4H2O (98 %), Co(CH3CO2)2·4H2O (99.99 %), SC(NH2)2 (99.0 %), HOCH2CH2OH (99.8 %) were procured from Merck Co. All materials employed in this work were prepared by Merck Co. and were used without any purification.

The ADAK DC power supply (from TDX POWER SERVICES) was used to synthesize CQDs through a simple two-electrode system [33]. Two graphite rods, each 5.6 mm, were placed into ultrapure water and exposed to a constant voltage of 30–40 V. For several days, the synthesis process was carried out with continuous stirring until a dark brown liquid was produced. After that, the mixture was filtered and centrifuged at 22,000 rpm in a high-speed tabletop centrifuge [34].

The N/S-RCQD@NiCo2S4 was synthesized by dissolving Co(CH3COO)2 (0.3 mmol) and Ni(CH3COO)2 (0.15 mmol) in 30 mL of CQDs/EG suspension agitating at 80 °C for 2 h. Then 0.9 mmol of thiourea was added to the above suspension, and moved the mixture to a 40 mL autoclave. The solvothermal reaction was monitored at 200 °C for 6 h. The resulting product was filtered, rinsed with a large amount of deionized water, and finally lyophilized. Similar to N/S-RCQD@NiCo2S4, N/S-RCQD@Co3S4 was created using the same procedure, with the except that the molar ratio of the metal precursor to thiourea was 0.5. NiCo2S4 was also synthesized under similar conditions for comparison.

FTIR spectra of CQDs and N/S-RCQD were carried out at the range of 500–4500 cm−1 by a Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscope (Tensor 27 from Germany Brulker Co). Field emission scanning electron microscopy, FESEM (TESCAN, MIRA III, Czech Republic) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) were used to examine the morphologies of electrodes at a 100 kV accelerating voltage. X-ray diffraction, XRD, was used to investigate the crystalline structures using a Cu lamp (k = 0.1541 nm) running at 40 kV and 30 mA with a Philips model PW1730. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using a SPECS FlexMod. The twin anode X-ray source XR 50, the monochromatic X-ray source FOCUS 500, and the small spot -FOCUS monochromator sources are all X-ray sources for XPS/ESCA. All the binding energy in the XPS analysis was corrected by setting the C 1s transition at 285.2 eV [35,36]. Furrier transform infra-red, FT-IR, spectroscopy was used to confirm the reduction of CQDs to RCQDs and C-N/S covalent bond formation. The UV–vis Analytic Jena Specord 250 spectrophotometer is used for recording the absorption spectrum of CQDs in the wavelength range of 200–800 nm. The photoluminescence excitation and photoluminescence emission spectra were prepared by LS-45 Fluorescence Spectrophotometer from PerkinElmer Co.

For all electrochemical experiments, an AUTOLAB PGSTAT30 potentiostat-galvanostat with a frequency response analyzer, FRA, the module was employed.The electrochemical manner of the electrodes was evaluated using linear sweep voltammetry (LSV), cyclic voltammetry, chronoamperometry and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). The electrode was rotated at 1400 rpm to avoid generating bubbles that could introduce to avoid the production of electrochemical noise during LSV and chronoamperometric measurements. Using the ZView® (II) program, the Nyquist plots were fitted to appropriate equivalent circuits. A working, counter, and reference electrode array comprised a glassy carbon electrode, GCE (d = 2 mm), a platinum plate, and an Ag/AgCl (3 M KCl). The working electrode was prepared as follows: to make a homogenous ink, 100 μL of a 5 wt% Nafion solution was mixed with 1 mL of a 4:1 v/v water/ethanol solution and 4 mg of the catalyst was ultrasonically dispersed in it in an ice water bath. The catalyst ink was then injected into a GC electrode with a diameter of 2 mm. The mass loading of catalysts on the GC electrode was 0.26 mg/cm2.

X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns of NiCo2S4, N/S-RCQD@NiCo2S4 and N/S-RCQD@Co3S4 are shown in Fig. 1a. The addition of CQDs to NiCo2S4 led to a change in the XRD pattern. As shown in Fig. 1a, a typical XRD pattern of N/S-RCQD@NiCo2S4 powder has been observed, which has sharper diffraction peaks than NiCo2S4. The sharper peaks in N/S-RCQD@NiCo2S4 can be attributed to CQD particles, which prevent the aggregation of NiCo2S4 bulk, resulting in a more distinct spectrum. Additionally, the N/S-RCQD@NiCo2S4 spectrum has shifted to larger angles than NiCo2S4's spectrum, which is explained by the Successful intercalation of the CQDs into the host matrix and the introduction of defects in the samples [37]. A low -intensity peak demonstrates the presence of RCQD carbon in N/S-RCQD@NiCo2S4 and N/S-RCQD@Co3S4 at 2θ = 22.15 o. At diffraction angles of (13.38°), (26.72°), (30.88°), (37.52°), (45.98°), (50.48°), and (55.18°), the characteristic peaks of NiCo2S4 belong to (111), (220), (311), (400), (422), (511), and (440), respectively. Also, the reported broad diffraction peaks at 16.78°, 26.78°, 30.84°, 38.74°, 49.06°, 50.96° and 54.72° correspond to the (111), (220), (311), (400), (422), (511) and (440) planes, respectively, in the XRD pattern of N/S-RCQD@Co3S4. The NiCo2S4 and N/S-RCQD@Co3S4 samples did not coincide with any standard data due to low crystallinity, although their agreement with previous similar works was satisfactory [38–41]. The N/S-RCQD@NiCo2S4 spectrum was in satisfactory agreement with the standard data of a single NiCo2S4 cubic spinel structure (PDF- 00-043-1477), except that the 2θ angles in the standard data were larger than in the N/S-RCQD@NiCo2S4 which could be related to non-ideal crystal formation in the presence of N/S-RCQD nanoparticles. Also, a trace of Fe metal impurity was detected in our synthesis based on standard data, which could be related to the precursor of used materials.

The composition of the N/S-RCQD@NiCo2S4 is further confirmed using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Fig. 1b displays the survey pattern of the N/S-RCQD@NiCo2S4 sample indicating the existence of Ni, Co, S, C, O and N elements. According to Fig. 1c, the six fitted peaks are shown in the Ni 2p spectrum of N/S-RCQD@NiCo2S4, with two peaks situated at 855.4 and 873.7 eV consistent with the Ni2+ signal, while the peaks at 857.7 and 881.7 eV agree with Ni3+ and two shake-up satellites (denoted as “Sat.”). Similarly, the Co 2p, the binding energies of two spin-orbit doublets at 778.8 and 797.5 eV, 781.4 and 801 eV, can be attributed to Co3+ and Co2+, respectively. The peaks at 782.5 and 803.4 eV are assigned to two shake-up satellites (Fig. 1d) [14]. Moreover, for the S 2p XPS spectrum exhibited in Fig. 1e, there are five peaks can be divided into the metal–sulfur bond peak at 161.3 eV of 2p3/2 and 162.7 eV of 2p1/2, thiolic-S peak at 164.1 eV which is relevant to the chemisorption of thiourea's breakdown product, thiophenic-S peak at 164.6 eV and SO4 2− peak at 168.5 eV [42,43]. According to Fig. 1f, the C 1s of the XPS spectra at 284.3, 285.2, 286.5, and 288.5 eV were tasked with the C-S-C, CC, C-N/C-S and CO peaks, respectively, indicating the successful doping of the N and S heteroatoms into the carbon matrix [38]. Fig. 1g displays the high-resolution O1s spectra for N/S-RCQD@NiCo2S4. Peaks of CO and C-OH/C-O-C bonds can be found in the composite, which are attributed to the O1s at 531.8 and 532.8 eV, respectively. In addition, a further peak at 531 eV for the C-O-M (M = Ni and Co) bond is detected, accompanied by a low content of C-OH and/or C-O-C groups, suggesting a replacement of the C-OH groups or a C-O-C group ring-opening reaction to form the C-O-M linkage [38,43]. Additionally, the N 1s XPS spectrum in Fig. 1h provides doping of N into the carbon matrix by showing the presence of CN peaks. Three kinds of N can be detected at peaks of 398.5, 399.5, and 400.8 eV attributed to pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, and graphitic N, respectively [38,42]. As shown in Fig. 2(a–c), the morphology of NiCo2S4, N/S-RCQD@Co3S4, and N/S-RCQD@ NiCo2S4 is investigated using a FESEM. Since metal ions self-assemble without support, the raw NiCo2S4 exhibited the cloud mass-like form in Fig. 2a. After introducing CQD, the morphologies of NiCo2S4 have changed considerably. As shown in Figs.2b and c, Co3S4 and NiCo2S4 have a flower-like with Wrinkled sheet edges. The sheet edges are uniformly distributed and cross-linked, increasing the contact area with the electrolyte, shortening the ion diffusion channels and boosting their electrochemical performance [44]. The shape of nanosheet edges in samples b and c is different with sample c nanosheets edges that are thinner. In Fig. 3c, the average thickness of these wrinkled nanosheet edges is 53.2 nm. The formation of this morphology increases the surface area of N/SRCQD@NiCo2S4 [45]. In addition to the effect of N/S-RCQDs, we can also mention the impact of Ni ions in the forming these nanostructure. The substitution of various cations with various intrinsic properties and ionic radii, mainly due to their distribution in octahedral and tetrahedral positions, causes unique properties such as colour, magnetic behaviour, catalytic activity, and optical features of spinel structures. Adding nickel to the catalyst has likely reduced the particle size, leading to the formation of thinner sheets and increased catalytic performance.

Ni, Co, O, N and C elements are uniformly distributed in the N/S-RCQD@NiCo2S4 hybrid, confirming the formation of a composite structure based on the EDS elemental mapping (Fig. 2(d–f)). The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) further analyzes the morphology of the N/S-RCQD@NiCo2S4 nanohybrid. The distribution of wrinkled nanosheet edges of the N/S-RCQD@NiCo2S4 was further confirmed by the HRTEM images with few layers. Based on the HRTEM images, which show the incorporation of cloud mass-like NiCo2S4 and nanoparticles, two scenarios are considered to be likely: The first case is that Wrinkled nanosheet edges are wrapped at the N/S-RCQD nanoparticles. The second case is that N/S-RCQD nanoparticles are spread on NiCo2S4 flower-like structures. Assuming the second case, we should have a simple combination of NiCo2S4 and CQDs, while having a different nanohybrid. Therefore, the second case is more likely. N/S-RCQDs with an average diameter of 6.5 ± 0.8 nm were wrapped at the nanosheet-like edges in the HRTEM image of N/S-RCQD@NiCo2S4 (In the inset of Fig. 3a). Moreover, the high magnification images of N/S- RCQD@NiCo2S4 evidenced the lattice fringes with an interplanar spacing of 0.28 nm, 0.22 nm and 0.2 nm respectively, corresponding to (311), (400) and (422) crystal planes, respectively, of the NiCo2S4 phase [46]. The HRTEM image of N/S-RCQD@NiCo2S4 nanohybrid confirms the formation of NiCo2S4 flower-like with the wrinkled nanosheets-like edges that are wrapped the N/S-RCQD nanoparticles [47]. Thus, we have a core-shell structure of N/S-RCQDs@NiCo2S4.

By using FTIR analysis, the surface functional groups of CQD and N/S-CQDs-RCQD were further confirmed, as seen in Fig. 4a. In terms of pure CQDs, there are several peaks situated at roughly 3253 (hydroxyl, OH), 2976 (CH), 1718 (carboxy, CO), 1589 (aromatic, CC), 1271 (C-O-C) and 1103 (CO) cm−1 [48]. The N/S-RCQD similarly displayed the peaks above in comparison to pure CQDs, with the exception that the intensity of the peaks (such as peak CO) has decreased, which demonstrates the excellent reduction of CQDs. The CN bending and CS stretching vibrations also coincide with two little distinctive peaks at 734 and 617 cm−1, respectively, indicating effective N and S atom incorporation into the N/S-RCQD [49]. To investigate the synthesis of CQDs, the UV–vis, excitation and photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra of CQDs are given in Fig. 4b. Based on UV–vis spectrum, CQDs exhibit two absorption peaks at about 230 and 350 nm. The apparent peak around 230 nm is related to the π-π* transitions of the sp2 electron orbitals in the aromatic CC bond. The second one absorbed into the first high peak and only visible as a shoulder, is connected n-π* transitions of the carbonyl functional groups [34]. The optical characteristics of the created CQDs are confirmed by the photoluminescent spectra. The optical characteristics of CQDs are verified by the presence of a PL peak at about 465 nm in the visible light range, where the excitation wavelength is fixed at 370 nm. Also emission wavelength –dependent excitation spectra at 465 nm further optical properties of synthesized CQDs. In addition, Fig. 4c shows the photoluminescence emission of CQD and N/S-RCQDs at the excitation wavelength of 370 nm. The intensity of photoluminescence emission has increased in the N/S-RCQD, implying a greater quantum yield. This result shows more electron transition in N/s-RCQDs, which improves the performance of the electrocatalyst for the OER process [50]. It is important to note that one can refer to our previous work to observe the TEM image of CQDs that were synthesized using this method [34].

Fig. 5a shows LSV curves to evaluate the OER electrocatalytic performance of N/S-RCQD@ NiCo2S4, N/S-RCQD@Co3S4, NiCo2S4 and GCE by considering the IR compensation. In 0.1 M KOH, NiCo2S4@N/S-RCQD has a significantly higher OER current density than all other samples. The NS-RCQD@NiCo2S4 catalyst has vigorous OER catalytic activity, with a ƞ10 (E10–1.23 V) of 390 mV., comparable to the best performance of the well-studied Co3O4/graphene hybrid and CoSe2/Mn3O4 catalyst stated at the same loadings [51,52]. In addition, the LSV curves of commercial IrO2 and RuO2 were represented for comparison. The value of ƞ10 for commercial IrO2 and RuO2 was 622 and 520 mV, respectively. The OER polarization curves (Fig. 5a) were transformed into Tafel plots, where the overpotential is displayed as a function of the logarithmic current density to gain a better understanding of the catalytic activity. Fig. 5b shows that the Tafel slopes of N/S-RCQD@NiCo2S4, N/S-RCQD@Co3S4 and NiCo2S4 are 85.6, 91.4 and 91.9 mV/dec, respectively, indicating the better OER activity of N/S-RCQD@NiCo2S4 than the other samples. The resulting Tafel slopes are almost greater than expected at low overpotential, which is reasonable by the creation of bubbles/oxygen species on the surface of the electrode. The generation of bubbles reduces the active surface area of the electrode and, at the same time, affects the ohmic resistance. Both of these causes have the potential to change polarization curves. To obtain relevant results, an adequate adjustment of polarization curves (ƞ = Eapp-(IR + 1.23)) was performed before performing the Tafel plot analysis [53,54]. In each sequence of water oxidation, metal sites experience continuous oxidation and reduction processes, and transition metal oxyhydroxide (MOOH) is the essential intermediate in the OER process, according to the transition metal oxide/sulfide OER mechanism catalyzed in alkali [38]. Here, the OER pathways are presented as outlined below [14].

where M is the transition metal. The adsorption of hydroxide ions on the catalyst's active site is the first step in this suggested mechanism. An active β-M(IV)-OOH is formed during this reaction, resulting in an efficient oxygen evolution reaction. We can state that large S2 anions arranged in dense arrays (larger than O2 anions) with Ni and Co metal present in the tetrahedral and octahedral sites, respectively, of the spinel crystal structure are responsible for the improvement in OER activity of the NiCo2S4. As a result, there are more Co(III) cation octahedral active sites for effective OER activity. Also, Ni enhances the transfer of electrons because of its high electron conductivity [14].

The exchange current density, I₀, values were obtained from the Tafel calculation for N/S-RCQD@NiCo2S4, N/S-RCQD@Co3S4 and NiCo2S4 are 0.0003004 mA/cm2, 0.0002313 mA/cm2 and 0.0000036 mA/cm2 respectively. Based on the mentioned mechanism, the presence of Ni and Co ions in the catalyst causes better performance of N/S-RCQD@NiCo2S4. In addition, carbon quantum dots interconnects nanosheets like-edges of NiCo2S4, increasing active sites and facilitating mass transfer [42]. Another kinetic parameter that identifies how quickly a catalyst may catalyze the desired electrochemical process is the catalytic turnover frequency (TOF). In the case of OER, TOF indicates the number of oxygen molecules evolved per active site per second, providing evidence to understand electrocatalysts' intrinsic activity. We use the following equation to calculate TOF:
(5)

Fig. 5c displays the TOF plots of N/S-RCQD@NiCo2S4/GC, N/S-RCQD@Co3S4/GC and NiCo2S4/GC electrodes. The TOF plots were represented in the 1.61–1.71 V/RHE. According to Fig. 5c, the maximum TOF value for N/s-RCQD@NiCo2S4 is 5 s−1 in this range, which is 4.5 and 71 times higher than N/S-RCQD@Co3S4/(1.1 s−1) and NiCo2S4 (0.07 s−1). This finding suggests that the intrinsic activity of N/S-RCQD@NiCo2S4 is higher than that of N/S-RCQD@Co3S4 and NiCo2S4. The TOF value obtained for N/S-RCQD@NiCo2S4 is higher compared to a similar case. NiCo2O4/Ti, at 1.65 V vs. RHE, got a TOF of 0.113 s−1 [57]. Table 1 shows the calculated TOF of the synthesized electrodes.

The temperature dependence of OER performance was used to obtain the OER activation energy better to evaluate the intrinsic activity of the synthesized catalysts. The Ea values can be calculated using the Arrhenius equation. [58]:
(8)
k
T
=
Ae
− Ea / kt
where k(T) is the rate constant, A is the pre-exponential factor, T is temperature, and R is the universal gases constant. The current density, I, is used as a metric of reaction kinetics in electrochemical reactions. Consequently, instead of using actual k (T), extending the Arrhenius equation for I values is typical by assuming that they are related to k (T). We may determine an apparent activation energy, Eapp, based on current densities recorded at various temperatures according to a modified Arrhenius equation for electrochemical reactions. (Fig. 6).
(9)
E app
= R
d
ln I
1 / T

The Eapp values were derived based on the plots' slope in Fig. 6d (slope = −Eapp/2.303R). The average Eapp values are also recorded in Table 2 . As shown in Fig. 6, with increasing temperature, the OER onset potential decreases and the current density increases in the anodic branch, which are enlarged in the inset of Fig. 6 a, b and c for clarity. The activation energy obtained for N/S-RCQD@ NiCo2S4 has the lowest value, and this result confirms the better performance of this catalyst, as mentioned earlier. This results from the introduction of Ni cations into the octahedral sites of the spinel crystal structure, which enhanced electrical conductivity and created new active sites with considerably reduced activation energy [59].

Furthermore, the presence of carbon quantum dots in the N/S-RCQD@NiCo2S4 structure results in the formation of wrinkled nanosheets-like- edges (SEM and HRTEM images), which improves electrical conductivity and thus facilitates charge transfer at the catalyst surface [59].

The electrochemically active surface area (ECSA) of the N/S-RCQD@NiCo2S4, N/S RCQD@ Co3S4 and NiCo2S4 electrodes in 0.1 M KOH was determined using the measured electrochemical double layer capacitance (Cdl). Using cyclic voltammetry, a potential range where no Faradaic reactions were visible was obtained (CV). The charging current ja – jc was determined using cyclic voltammograms at various scan rates at the potential of 0.397 V vs. Ag/AgCl, as illustrated in Figs. 7a, b, c. Cdl can be estimated the slope of the ja-jc vs. ν straight line plot (Fig. 7d), using the formula ja – jc = 2 ν Cdl (where ja, jc and ν are anode current density, cathode current density and scan rate, respectively) [65]. It has been discovered that N/S-RCQD@ NiCo2S4 has a Cdl value of 2.1 mF/cm2, which is much higher than N/S-RCQD@Co3S4 (1.2 mF/cm2) and NiCo2S4 (0.2 mF/cm2), respectively. When compared to Co3S4@N/S-RCQD and NiCo2S4, this implies that N/S-RCQD@ NiCo2S4 has a large specific surface area and a large number of active sites, which is one of the critical parameters in enhancing OER activity [66].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of the durability of the electrocatalysts by chronoamperometry technique
ce:section.ce:para: The stability of the electrodes is one of the most important aspects of catalytic activity. Thus, chronoamperometry test were performed in 0.10 M KOH solution at a constant anodic potential of 0.7 V vs. Ag/AgCl and 1400 rpm for the 24 h (Fig. 8 ). The initial current density observed was 55.7 mA/cm2 (in t = 0), which was diminished to 33.4 mA/cm2 (in t = 24 h). Thus current density loss was 40 % in N/S-RCQD@NiCo2S4. The improved catalytic performance of the NiCo2S4 electrode in the presence of RCQDs and the synergistic effect between nickel and cobalt nanoparticles with RCQDs lead to the steady state current density of N/S-RCQD@NiCo2S4/GCE. We may further deduce that N/S-RCQD@NiCo2S4 durability is due to the stable framework of the heteroatom-doped carbon quantum dot [67,68].

To assess the electron transfer kinetic parameters of the produced electrodes, EIS measurements were carried out in an alkaline medium with a perturbation voltage of 10.0 mV (rms) sinusoidal voltage in the frequency range of 100 kHz to 100 mHz at three applied voltages. The potentials were chosen based on the LSV results. The Nyquist plots of N/S-RCQD@NiCo2S4/GCE, N/S-RCQD@Co3S4/GCE and NiCo2S4 at three anodic voltages are introduced in Fig. 9a, b and c. The OER Nyquist plots reveal two separate semicircles: The high-frequency region is due to the resistance of the ionic conducting routes in the pores filled with the electrolyte, which is caused by the porous structure of the active material. The last semicircle in the low-frequency area of the OER Nyquist plot displays the charge transfer restriction in the electrode/electrolyte interface. [69]. Fig. 9d shows the equivalent electrical circuit utilized to fit the EIS spectra. The fitting results are presented in Table 3 . The resistance of film porous structure Rf should ideally be free of potential; nevertheless, EIS fitting findings (shown in Table 3) showed a modest drop in the resistance of the first RC loop as potentially increased, assisting in raising the total OER rate as potentially increased. In contrast, Rct is significantly sensitive to potential, implying that the second RC loop is associated to charge transfer constraints [69]. The interconnected nanosheet-like-edges network of N/S-RCQD@NiCo2S4 exhibits the lowest Rct, demonstrating improved electron and charge transfer abilities at the electrode/electrolyte interface. Due to the frequency dispersion of the capacitance at the electrode/solution interface, a constant phase element, CPE, was used instead of pure capacitors for fitting the EIS data [70]. The admittance, Y0, and the power, n, of the subsequent equation are used to define and connect CPE to Cdl [71]:
(10)
C dl
=
Y 0
Rct
1 / n
/ Rct

The Cf is created due to the concentration gradient of positive and negative ions, and the Faraday reaction does not occur. Therefore, its value changes slightly. While the Cdl, which is formed due to the Faradaic reaction in the electrode-electrolyte interface, increases with increasing potential because the amount of charge exchange at the interface of the electrolyte electrode increases. The Nyquist curve's diameter reveals that the hybrid material N/S-RCQD@NiCo2S4 has the lowest charge transfer resistance and the maximum activity for OER. Furthermore, the EIS result of OER demonstrates that N and S co-doping improves the electrical structure of carbon quantum dots while lowering resistance [38].

The one-pot solvothermal process was used to successfully create N/S-RCQD@NiCo2S4 nanocomposite material with core-shell structure. The synergistic impact of N/S-RCQD and NiCo2S4, as well as N, S co doping, can significantly enhance the conductivity and electronic microstructure of N/S-RCQD@NiCo2S4 thereby improving electron transport and electrocatalytic activity. With a few loadings of 0.26 mg/cm2 in alkaline pH, N/S-RCQD@NiCo2S4 exhibits remarkable OER catalytic activity with a modest 0.39 V at a current density of 10 mA cm−2, high anodic currents, and a Tafel slope of 85.6 mVdec−1. In addition, the TOF value of N/S-RCQD@NiCo2S4 in 1.66 V is 5 s−1 implying to high ECSA and desirable OER performance. The specific activity of each electrocatalyst was also assessed utilizing the measured electrochemical double layer capacitance (Cdl) as a secondary screen of OER activity. The largest double layer capacitance was found in N/S-RCQD@NiCo2S4 indicating that it has a higher intrinsic activity than the other systems studied. According to the EIS findings, N/S-RCQD@NiCo2S4 has the lowest interface charge transfer resistance, implying that N/S-RCQD@NiCo2S4 has the fastest charge transfer process during the OER process of all the materials studied. As a result, N/S-RCQD@NiCo2S4 can be used as a promising electrocatalyst for future research into electrocatalysts with high overall performance.

Mahdiyyeh Sadeghi Amjadi: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Visualization. Habib Ashassi-Sorkhabi: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Mir Ghasem Hosseini: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Bruno G. Pollet: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Elnaz Asghari: Supervision, Data curation, Writing – review & editing.
